summary: Simon Späti explores how to achieve flow states and deep work in an age of digital distractions, drawing from personal experience as a self-employed author. He argues that tech companies manipulate us like lab animals through dopamine-driven engagement, while artists who focus on process over reward naturally enter flow states. The post offers practical strategies for creating focus-friendly environments, the importance of breaks and rest, and embracing slow productivity. He advocates for 'deep happiness' from mastery and meaningful work over 'shallow happiness' from quick dopamine hits, ultimately connecting flow to living a deeper, more fulfilling life.
key_points: Tech platforms manipulate behavior through dopamine rewards similar to how researchers condition lab animals, while artists escape this by finding reward in the creative process itself
Environment design is crucial for flow: dedicated browsers, offline tools like Obsidian, changing locations every 1.5-2.5 hours, and repetitive non-vocal music signal 'work time' to the brain

Breaks and rest are essential ingredients often overlooked—including vacations, good sleep (especially REM after 7-8 hours), and different types of rest (physical, mental, creative, emotional, social, spiritual, sensory)
Cal Newport's slow productivity principles (do fewer things, work at natural pace, obsess over quality) lead to a 'deeper life' that is more fulfilled and present
To unblock creativity, change the medium (writing to talking, digital to paper), add constraints, and practice doing nothing—unlimited freedom actually blocks creativity
money_quotes: Aren't they the same as the researcher, feeding us with dopamine to tell us to do what they want?
The drawing, the way to the end, is the reward, not the likes or selling the art.
Writing in the flow state, as I'm writing these words right now when I am merely in control, but the words flow out of my fingers. I guide them, but I do not write or choose the words.
There is no talking block, only writer's block.
Unlimited freedom is super-blocking, especially for creativity.
Writing an article or learning something hard, like coding or vim motions, is initially hard. But the reward is long-term. We get deep satisfaction from being in the flow.
I think of my creative process as gardening. Things inside us build up slowly, but great things will come out with constant nurturing.
When I don't have inspiration or I have a block, I do nothing. I live.
key_insight: True productivity and creativity come not from optimizing for external rewards and constant stimulation, but from designing environments and habits that support flow states and embracing the slow, patient process of deep work.
